name: Royal Free NHS-DeepMind Streams Deployment
description: Clinical deployment of the Streams application within Royal Free London NHS workflows for acute kidney injury alerting and response.

evidence_manifest.deployment_name: Streams deployment at Royal Free London NHS Foundation Trust

sources.title: NHS and technology leaders agree groundbreaking partnership to improve safety and save lives
sources.publisher: Royal Free London NHS Foundation Trust

sources.title: Statement re DeepMind data processing agreement during testing phase of Streams app
sources.publisher: Royal Free London NHS Foundation Trust

sources.title: Royal Free London publishes findings of legal audit in use of Streams

transparency_explainability.claim: Public statements disclosed deployment purpose and agreement boundaries, while technical internals remained limited in public disclosures.

fairness_nondiscrimination.claim: The deployment targeted acute kidney injury workflows and did not claim explicit demographic fairness optimization.

safety_robustness.claim: The deployment aimed to improve timely clinical response and was embedded in routine care settings with documented operational collaboration.

privacy_data_governance.claim: Public updates emphasized stricter contractual constraints and regulator-visible governance expectations for patient data handling.
privacy_data_governance.scoring_impact: keeps privacy score below high range

human_agency_oversight.claim: Clinical teams retained responsibility for diagnosis and treatment while Streams operated as workflow support.

accountability.claim: Legal-audit publication and clarified governance agreements created explicit accountability and external scrutiny pathways.

assumptions: Scores convert documented deployment and governance evidence into MEDF's Likert representation.
Evidence is interpreted for governance evaluation rather than clinical efficacy benchmarking.
The case is treated as a real hospital deployment with durable source provenance metadata.
